items.prompt: A function f is continuous at a point c if:
items.answer: the limit of f(x) as x tends to c equals f(c)

expected.correct: the limit of f(x) as x tends to c equals f(c)
options: the limit of f(x) as x tends to c equals f(c)

items.prompt: A real function f is called continuous if it is continuous at:
items.answer: every point in the domain of f

expected.correct: every point in the domain of f
options: every point in the domain of f

items.prompt: If f and g are continuous at c, the quotient f/g is continuous at c provided:
The composite f o g is continuous at c if g is continuous at c and f is continuous at:
The derivative of f at c is defined as the limit, as h tends to 0, of:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch05 Sec 5.3 derivative definition

items.prompt: At how many points is f(x) = |x| not differentiable?
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch05 Sec 5.3 (|x| not differentiable at 0)

items.prompt: Give an example showing a continuous function need not be differentiable, and state the correct one-way relationship between differentiability and continuity.

expected.rubric: f(x) = |x| is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at x = 0 (the left and right derivatives are -1 and +1). The correct relationship is one-way: every differentiable function is continuous, but a continuous function need not be differentiable.
markPoints: Example: |x| is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at 0
Reason: the one-sided derivatives at 0 differ (-1 and +1)
Differentiable implies continuous, but not the converse

items.prompt: Using the chain rule, the derivative of f(x) = sin(x^2) is:
When differentiating an implicit relation in x and y with respect to x, each term in y must be differentiated using:
items.answer: the chain rule, introducing a factor dy/dx

expected.correct: the chain rule, introducing a factor dy/dx
options: the chain rule, introducing a factor dy/dx
no special rule; treat y as a constant

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch05 Sec 5.3.2 implicit functions

items.prompt: The derivative of sin-inverse x with respect to x is:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch05 Sec 5.3.3 inverse trig derivatives

items.prompt: The range of the exponential function y = b^x (b > 1) is:
items.answer: the set of all positive real numbers

expected.correct: the set of all positive real numbers
options: the set of all positive real numbers

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch05 Sec 5.4 Definition 3 / features

items.prompt: Since 2^3 = 8, the value of log_2 8 is:
The derivative of log x (natural log) with respect to x is:
Logarithmic differentiation is most useful for differentiating functions of the form:
items.answer: y = (f(x))^(g(x)) with variable base and exponent

expected.correct: y = (f(x))^(g(x)) with variable base and exponent
options: y = (f(x))^(g(x)) with variable base and exponent

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch05 Sec 5.5 Logarithmic differentiation

items.prompt: For a curve given parametrically by x = f(t), y = g(t), dy/dx equals:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch05 Sec 5.6 parametric form

items.prompt: If y = x^3, then the second order derivative d2y/dx2 equals:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch05 Sec 5.7 second order derivative

items.prompt: Explain what the second order derivative of y with respect to x is and how you compute it for y = f(x).

expected.rubric: The second order derivative is the derivative of the first derivative: d/dx(dy/dx), denoted d2y/dx2 or f''(x). To compute it, first find the first derivative dy/dx (using whatever rule applies - chain, implicit, parametric, etc.), then differentiate that result again with respect to x.
markPoints: It is the derivative of the first derivative, d/dx(dy/dx) = d2y/dx2 = f''(x)
Step 2: differentiate dy/dx again with respect to x

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch05 Sec 5.7 second order derivative
